tags.qa_notes: RE-AUDITED 2026-06-25. El Faro (lighthouse) at western tip of Isla Canales de Afuera. Rocky reef descending to 30m. divemasteraustin.com confirms max 64ft/19m (not 30m) — note: multiple sources say 30m; the 19m figure may be the typical working depth. Retained 30m as max listed by panamadivecenter.com. Bull sharks and oceanic blacktips confirmed by panamadivecenter.com. Bigeye jacks, dog snappers, barracuda, scorpionfish, octopus, seahorse, frogfish confirmed. Manta rays seasonal. Whale sharks seasonal (December-April peak). Garden eels on sandy sections confirmed. Strong variable currents confirmed. Three independent sources all confirm this site.

UNRESOLVED. 2026-06-25 re-audit: No independent dive operator, ScubaBoard thread, or dive database (PADI, panamadivecenter.com, scubacoiba.com, divemasteraustin.com, coiba-island.com, thearkdivers.com, divebasecoiba.com) lists a Coiba National Park dive site by this name. 'Machete Caves' is in Bocas del Toro on Panama's Caribbean coast — a different location. Known Coiba wall/reef sites near these coordinates include sites around Isla Coibita but none called 'Machete Point'. Validated=false. Do not publish until confirmed by a local operator.

RE-AUDITED 2026-06-25. Islet NE of Coiba Island. Coords verified: 7.5933/-81.7124 (snorkeling-report.com: 7.593368/-81.713267). Shallow reef 5-18m around volcanic islet. Intermediate difficulty confirmed (not Advanced). Day-trip accessible from Santa Catalina. Marine life confirmed by 3 sources: hawksbill and green turtles, whitetip reef sharks (deeper rocky sections), king angelfish, surgeonfish (razor surgeonfish), grunts, snapper, moray eels, pufferfish (white-spotted puffer, spot-fin porcupinefish), parrotfish, wrasses, triggerfish. No pelagic megafauna (bull sharks, hammerheads) confirmed at this specific site. Aquarium-like density of species confirmed.

RE-AUDITED 2026-06-25. Near Isla Rancheria. Cathedral-like rock walls, overhangs, channels. Depth range: panamadivecenter.com lists 12-30m; divemasteraustin.com confirms 18m/58ft max working depth with most diving at 20-40ft. NOTE: divemasteraustin confirms large manta ray sighting here. Baby octopus confirmed (divemasteraustin). Marine life confirmed: whitetip reef sharks, moray eels, lobsters, snappers, jacks, angelfish, parrotfish, wrasse, scorpionfish, frogfish, seahorses, turtles, octopus, barracuda, manta rays. Oceanic whitetip claim removed (not confirmed at this specific site). Antler coral claim removed (no source confirmation). Three independent sources confirmed.

RE-AUDITED 2026-06-25. Bay on east side of Isla Coiba. '135 hectares (335 acres) — second largest coral reef in the Eastern Pacific' confirmed by coibanationalpark.com. CAVEAT: This claim should be noted as sourced from a single advocacy site; independent verification from a scientific source was not found. The '5,000 years old' reef claim originates from coibanationalpark.com; not independently verified — retained with attribution. The '33 shark species' and '800 fish species' figures apply to the entire Coiba National Park, NOT to Bahia Damas specifically — these are park-wide figures and have been noted accordingly in the description. Marine life confirmed at or adjacent to Bahia Damas: large schools of snapper, jacks, barracuda, tuna; manta rays; reef sharks; dolphins and whales (seasonal/adjacent waters). Whale sharks and humpback whales are seasonal park-wide visitors, not guaranteed at this bay specifically.

RE-AUDITED 2026-06-25. Isla Canal de Afuera — island with multiple dive areas. Primary sites: calm leeward coral gardens (snapper, jacks, surgeonfish, angelfish, parrotfish, moray eels, turtles) and exposed western point (Faro/El Faro — listed separately as Faro Reef). panamadivecenter.com confirms site list includes Wahoo Rock, Buffet, Faro, Bajo Pinon, Punta Deseo, La Lengua within the Canales area. 1.5-hour boat from Santa Catalina confirmed. ScubaBoard confirms rocky reef with encrusting sponges, orange cup corals, green/yellow finger-sponges, gorgonians, and hard corals. Species confirmed: snapper, jacks, surgeonfish, angelfish, parrotfish, moray eels, turtles (calm side); bull sharks, oceanic blacktips, bigeye jacks, dog snappers, barracuda, scorpionfish, octopus, seahorses, frogfish (exposed western areas). Three independent sources confirmed.

RE-AUDITED 2026-06-25. PADI confirms this site at southern end of Coiba National Park adjacent to Isla Jicarón. Described as 'islands on the drop-off' with seafloor plunging to thousands of meters. PADI species list confirmed: hammerhead sharks, barracuda, batfish, eagle rays, eels, flounders, groupers, lobsters, manta rays, moray eels, parrotfish, pipefish, porcupinefish, pufferfish, reef sharks, sea turtles, stingrays, yellowtail snapper. NOTE: species list covers Jicaron and Jicarita together as a combined dive area — individual site-specific observations are not separable from source. Accessible only on special trips, not standard day-trip routes. Three independent sources confirmed.

RE-AUDITED 2026-06-25. Coords corrected from (7.615, -81.735) to divemasteraustin.com-confirmed 7.632914/-81.781273. Hard coral reef, primarily 20-30ft (6-9m) average with 66ft (20m) max (divemasteraustin.com). divemasteraustin.com confirms: 'aquarium-like fields of hard coral' description; scorpionfish specifically mentioned including a mated pair observed. Used as rest dive between demanding sites. Difficulty Intermediate confirmed. Marine life: hard corals, scorpionfish (confirmed), diverse reef fish schools. No shark or ray encounters confirmed at this specific site — those are for more exposed sites. Three independent sources confirmed.

RE-AUDITED 2026-06-25. Coords verified: PADI dive site database 7.906614/-81.816730. CORRECTION: 'Montana Rusa' translates literally to 'Russian Mountain' in Spanish (montaña = mountain, rusa = Russian/feminine adjective) — NOT 'Roller Coaster' as stated in previous markdown. The site is nicknamed 'roller coaster' by divers due to the experience of riding currents, but the literal name means Russian Mountain. Markdown updated. Depth: PADI confirms 14m shallowest to 42m (140ft) max. divemasteraustin.com confirms 25m/83ft max on this visit. Retained 40m as the typical published maximum. Marine life confirmed: hammerhead sharks (seasonal, April-July peak; also documented sightings in September — ScubaBoard 2024 report); bull sharks, whitetip reef sharks, giant manta rays, large schools of bigeye trevally, barracuda, dog snappers, groupers. divemasteraustin.com confirms: oceanic blacktips, amberjacks. Strong to severe currents confirmed. Four independent sources.

UNRESOLVED. 2026-06-25 re-audit: No operator website (panamadivecenter.com, scubacoiba.com, divemasteraustin.com, coiba-island.com, thearkdivers.com, divebasecoiba.com), PADI dive site database, or ScubaBoard thread lists a Coiba National Park site named 'Shark Point' or 'Shark Point Coiba'. Coordinates (7.52, -81.75) are near central-west Coiba. Established shark-focused sites in Coiba are named El Faro (Punta Faro), Montana Rusa, Wahoo Rock, and Punta Peligro. Validated=false. Do not publish until confirmed by a local operator or replaced with a verified site.
